title: Engineering Coding Standards Guide
content:
All engineers at Meridian Consulting are expected to follow our established coding standards to ensure consistency, readability, and maintainability across all client projects. For JavaScript and TypeScript projects, we use ESLint with our custom Meridian ruleset (available at internal.meridian.dev/eslint-config-meridian) and enforce Prettier formatting with 2-space indentation, single quotes, and a print width of 100 characters. Python projects follow PEP 8 with Black formatting at a line length of 88 characters. All functions and classes must include docstrings or JSDoc comments explaining parameters, return values, and thrown exceptions. Variable names must be descriptive and avoid abbreviations shorter than 4 characters unless they are widely accepted conventions like 'id', 'url', or 'api'.

Commit messages must follow the Conventional Commits specification (conventionalcommits.org) with types including feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, and chore. Subject lines are limited to 72 characters, written in imperative mood, and must not end with a period. Branches should be named using the pattern `type/ticket-id-short-description`, for example `feat/MRD-412-user-authentication-flow`. All code must pass automated linting and formatting checks in CI before a pull request can be marked ready for review. Engineers are encouraged to run `npm run lint:fix` or `black .` locally before committing to reduce CI feedback loops. These standards are reviewed and updated quarterly by the Engineering Standards Committee, with the next review scheduled for April 1, 2025.

title: Technology Stack Selection Guidelines
content:
Meridian Consulting maintains a curated list of approved technologies to streamline onboarding, reduce operational overhead, and ensure our engineers can support each other across projects. Our primary backend languages are Python 3.11+ and Node.js 20 LTS. For frontend work, we standardize on React 18 with TypeScript 5.x. Relational data storage defaults to PostgreSQL 16, and Redis 7 is our standard for caching and session management. Infrastructure is managed through Terraform 1.6+ and deployed on AWS. Containerization uses Docker with orchestration via Amazon ECS for most projects, with Amazon EKS reserved for workloads requiring more granular Kubernetes control. Message queuing uses Amazon SQS for simple cases and Amazon MSK (Managed Kafka) for high-throughput event streaming.

When a project requires a technology not on the approved list, engineers must submit a Technology Adoption Request (TAR) through the internal portal at least 10 business days before project kickoff. The TAR is reviewed by the CTO, a senior engineer from the project team, and a representative from the security team. Factors evaluated include long-term maintainability, licensing costs, security track record, and existing team expertise. Technologies granted a one-project exception are tagged as 'provisional' and must be re-evaluated after project completion before being added to the standard stack. The full approved stack list, including version pinning guidelines and known end-of-life dates, is maintained at internal.meridian.dev/tech-stack and is updated on the first Monday of each month.

title: API Design and Guidelines
content:
All APIs built at Meridian Consulting must adhere to RESTful principles unless the use case explicitly justifies GraphQL or gRPC, both of which require TAR approval. REST endpoints use lowercase, hyphenated resource names in plural form (e.g., `/api/v1/user-accounts`) and must be versioned from the first release. API versions are communicated in the URL path. JSON is the required request and response format. HTTP status codes must be used semantically: 200 for success, 201 for resource creation, 204 for successful deletion with no body, 400 for client errors with a descriptive error object, 401 for unauthenticated requests, 403 for unauthorized requests, 404 for missing resources, and 500 for unexpected server errors. Error responses must include a `code` (machine-readable string), `message` (human-readable), and an optional `details` array.

All public-facing APIs must be documented using OpenAPI 3.1 specification files maintained in the same repository as the service code. Documentation is auto-generated and published to our internal developer portal at devportal.meridian.dev upon each merge to the main branch. APIs must implement rate limiting with a default of 1,000 requests per minute per client token, configurable per client. Pagination for list endpoints must use cursor-based pagination with a default page size of 25 and a maximum of 100. Authentication uses OAuth 2.0 with JWT access tokens issued by our centralized auth service. All tokens expire after 15 minutes, with refresh tokens valid for 30 days. Breaking changes to any API require a minimum 90-day deprecation notice communicated through the API changelog and direct client notification.

title: Development Environment Setup Procedures
content:
All engineers joining Meridian Consulting should complete their local development environment setup within the first two days of onboarding. Our standard setup script is available at `github.com/meridian-internal/dev-setup` and supports macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel) and Ubuntu 22.04 LTS. The script installs Homebrew or apt dependencies, nvm (Node Version Manager) targeting Node 20 LTS, pyenv targeting Python 3.11.6, Docker Desktop 4.x, the AWS CLI v2, and the Meridian CLI tool (`mcli`). Engineers should run `./setup.sh` and follow the prompts; total setup time is approximately 25 minutes on a standard MacBook Pro M2.

After the base environment is configured, each project repository contains a `README.md` with project-specific setup steps. Most projects include a `docker-compose.yml` that spins up all required local dependencies including databases, caches, and mock third-party services. Engineers run `mcli env start <project-name>` to pull the latest approved environment variables from AWS Secrets Manager into a local `.env` file — never commit `.env` files to version control. Pre-commit hooks are installed automatically via Husky and run linting, formatting checks, and unit tests on staged files before each commit. If a hook fails, the commit is blocked and the engineer must resolve the issue first. For troubleshooting, the #dev-setup Slack channel is monitored by at least two senior engineers during business hours (9 AM–6 PM EST, Monday–Friday). A full troubleshooting FAQ is maintained at internal.meridian.dev/dev-setup-faq.

title: Sprint Process and Ceremonies
content:
Meridian engineering teams operate in two-week sprints aligned to a Monday start and Friday end cadence. Sprint planning occurs every other Monday from 10:00–11:30 AM EST. The team reviews the prioritized backlog, refined in the preceding Thursday's backlog grooming session (30 minutes, attendance required for all engineers on the project). During planning, stories are estimated using Fibonacci points (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13) with a team velocity baseline recalculated as the rolling average of the last three sprints. No single story should exceed 8 points; anything larger must be broken down before entering a sprint. Each engineer's individual sprint capacity accounts for meetings, code reviews, and standard 10% buffer for unplanned work.

Daily standups are asynchronous by default, posted in the project's dedicated Slack channel by 10:00 AM in the engineer's local timezone using the format: what I completed yesterday, what I'm working on today, and any blockers. Synchronous standups (15 minutes via Zoom) are held on Mondays and Wednesdays for teams that prefer the cadence. Sprint demos occur on the last Friday of each sprint from 2:00–3:00 PM EST and are open to all Meridian employees and optionally to client stakeholders. Retrospectives immediately follow demos from 3:00–4:00 PM and are facilitated by the Scrum Master on a rotating quarterly basis. Action items from retros are tracked in Linear under the label `retro-action` and must be assigned an owner and due date before the meeting ends.

title: Code Review Policy and Standards
content:
All code changes at Meridian Consulting must be submitted as pull requests (PRs) and approved by at least two engineers before merging to the main branch. For changes to infrastructure-as-code or security-sensitive modules, one of the two approvals must come from a senior engineer (L4 or above) or the designated security champion for that project. PRs should be kept small and focused — the target is under 400 lines changed, excluding auto-generated files. Large PRs must be broken into a stacked PR series when possible. The PR description template (auto-populated by our GitHub repository template) requires a summary of changes, link to the Linear ticket, testing evidence (screenshots or test output), and a checklist confirming linting passes, tests pass, and documentation is updated.

Reviewers are expected to respond to PR review requests within one business day. If a reviewer cannot complete their review in time, they must reassign or notify the PR author via Slack so coverage can be arranged. Review comments should be categorized using our prefix conventions: `nit:` for non-blocking stylistic preferences, `question:` for clarification requests, `suggestion:` for optional improvements, and `blocker:` for issues that must be resolved before approval. Blocking issues must be addressed and the PR re-requested for review; the author should not resolve reviewer threads on behalf of the reviewer. Once all blocking comments are resolved and two approvals are received, the author merges using 'Squash and Merge' to maintain a clean linear history on the main branch. Direct pushes to main are blocked by branch protection rules on all production repositories.

title: Testing Standards and Requirements
content:
Meridian Consulting requires all production code to meet minimum testing coverage thresholds enforced in CI. Unit test coverage must be at or above 80% for all new code, measured by Istanbul (JavaScript) or pytest-cov (Python). Integration tests are required for all database interactions, external API calls, and message queue producers and consumers. End-to-end (E2E) tests using Playwright are required for all user-facing web application features and must cover the primary happy path and at least two error states per feature. Test files must be co-located with the source files they test, following the convention `component.test.ts` or `test_module.py`. Mocking third-party services in unit tests is required; real network calls in unit tests are prohibited and will cause CI to fail via our network interception layer.

Performance tests are required for any API endpoint expected to handle more than 500 requests per minute in production. We use k6 for load testing, with scripts stored in the `/tests/performance` directory of each repository. Performance benchmarks (P50, P95, P99 latencies and error rates) must be documented in the PR description and compared against the existing baseline. Security-related code paths, including authentication, authorization, input validation, and data encryption, require explicit test cases for both correct behavior and known attack vectors such as SQL injection and XSS. Test infrastructure costs are tracked per project and reviewed monthly; if test suite runtime exceeds 15 minutes in CI, the team must optimize before adding new tests. The Engineering Standards Committee reviews testing requirements annually, with the last review completed November 12, 2024.

title: CI/CD Pipeline Procedures
content:
Meridian Consulting uses GitHub Actions as the standard CI/CD platform. All repositories must include a `.github/workflows/ci.yml` pipeline that runs on every pull request and push to main. The CI pipeline stages are: (1) dependency installation and caching, (2) linting and formatting checks, (3) unit tests with coverage reporting, (4) integration tests, (5) security scanning via Snyk and Trivy, and (6) Docker image build and vulnerability scan. The full pipeline must complete in under 12 minutes; teams are alerted via Slack if pipeline duration exceeds this threshold for three consecutive runs. Pipeline failures block merging and the responsible engineer must address failures within 4 hours during business hours or before the next morning if discovered after 5 PM EST.

The CD pipeline deploys automatically to the staging environment on every merge to main, with a mandatory 10-minute smoke test suite running post-deployment. Promotion to production requires a manual approval step in GitHub Actions, which can only be granted by engineers with the `prod-deploy` role — currently held by all L4+ engineers and the DevOps lead, Marcus Chen. Production deployments are restricted to Tuesday through Thursday between 10 AM and 3 PM EST to minimize risk and ensure support coverage. Emergency hotfix deployments outside this window require approval from the on-call engineer and the engineering manager. All deployments are logged in the #deployments Slack channel automatically with the deploying engineer's name, commit SHA, change summary, and deployment duration. Rollback procedures are documented at internal.meridian.dev/ci-cd-rollback and must be tested in staging quarterly.

title: Knowledge Sharing and Documentation Practices
content:
Meridian Consulting invests in a culture of continuous learning and knowledge sharing as a core engineering practice. Each team holds a bi-weekly Engineering Lunch & Learn on the first and third Wednesday of each month from 12:00–1:00 PM EST. Engineers are encouraged to present for 20–30 minutes on any technically relevant topic, including post-mortems, new library evaluations, architectural experiments, or lessons learned from client engagements. Presentations are recorded and uploaded to our internal knowledge base at knowledge.meridian.dev within 48 hours. All engineers are expected to present at least twice per year, and presentation history is tracked as part of the engineering performance review process.

Beyond presentations, every engineering project must maintain a living Architecture Decision Record (ADR) log, updated within five business days of any significant technical decision. Runbooks for operational procedures must be written or updated before a new feature goes to production. Our internal wiki at knowledge.meridian.dev is organized by domain (frontend, backend, infrastructure, security, and client-specific spaces) and each section has a designated maintainer responsible for quarterly audits of stale content. Pages not updated in 12 months are flagged for review or archival. Engineers are also encouraged to contribute to the #til (Today I Learned) Slack channel for quick knowledge sharing. Monthly, the most upvoted TIL posts are compiled into our Engineering Newsletter, distributed to all 50 staff members on the last Friday of the month.

title: Security Practices and Requirements
content:
Security is a shared responsibility across the Meridian engineering team, not solely the domain of dedicated security staff. All engineers complete security awareness training annually through our KnowBe4 subscription, with a minimum passing score of 85% on the assessment. Project-specific security reviews are mandatory for any system handling personally identifiable information (PII), payment data, or protected health information (PHI). These reviews are conducted by our security champion rotation — a role held for three-month terms by senior engineers who have completed the OWASP Top 10 training module. Security review findings are categorized as Critical (must fix before launch), High (must fix within 7 days of launch), Medium (must fix within 30 days), and Low (fix within the next sprint).

All secrets — including API keys, database credentials, and certificates — must be stored in AWS Secrets Manager or AWS Parameter Store and never hardcoded or committed to version control. Pre-commit hooks scan for secret patterns using detect-secrets and will block commits containing potential credentials. Dependencies are scanned weekly by Snyk; any critical CVE must be patched within 48 hours, high CVEs within 7 days. Engineers must use MFA on all company accounts including GitHub, AWS, and GSuite. SSH keys for server access must be at least 4096-bit RSA or Ed25519, rotated annually, and the corresponding public key must be registered in our centralized key management system by the IT administrator. All production access events are logged to our CloudTrail audit trail, retained for 13 months.

title: Architecture Decision Record Template
content:
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) at Meridian Consulting use a standardized format to ensure consistency and searchability across all projects. Each ADR is numbered sequentially per project (e.g., ADR-0023 for the Hartwell Financial project) and stored in the `/docs/adr/` directory of the relevant repository. The required sections are: Title, Date, Status (Proposed / Accepted / Deprecated / Superseded), Context (the technical and business situation driving the decision), Decision (what was decided), Consequences (both positive and negative outcomes expected), Alternatives Considered (at least two alternatives with brief rationale for rejection), and Participants (names and roles of engineers involved in the decision). ADRs must be written within five business days of the decision being finalized.

For example, ADR-0023 for the Hartwell Financial engagement (accepted October 3, 2024) documents the decision to use Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL Serverless v2 instead of a provisioned RDS instance. Context included unpredictable workload patterns from Hartwell's batch-heavy reporting jobs, a projected 40% cost saving at low utilization, and the client's requirement for zero operational management overhead. The decision was made by lead engineer Priya Nair, architect David Tello, and the client's CTO. Alternatives considered included Provisioned RDS with read replicas (rejected due to over-provisioning cost) and CockroachDB (rejected due to insufficient team expertise and TAR timeline constraints). ADRs that affect cross-project infrastructure must be reviewed by the CTO before the status changes from Proposed to Accepted. All ADRs are indexed and searchable in the internal knowledge base under the project's dedicated space.

title: Incident Response Playbook
content:
When a production incident is detected — whether by automated alerting, client report, or engineer observation — the on-call engineer must acknowledge the PagerDuty alert within 10 minutes. If unacknowledged, the escalation path is: on-call engineer → secondary on-call → engineering manager → CTO. Upon acknowledgment, the incident is assigned a severity level: SEV1 (complete service outage or data breach, all-hands response), SEV2 (significant feature degradation affecting >20% of users), SEV3 (partial degradation, workaround available), and SEV4 (minor issue, no immediate user impact). For SEV1 and SEV2, a dedicated Slack channel is created immediately with the format `#inc-YYYYMMDD-short-description` and all relevant engineers are summoned. A dedicated incident commander role is assumed by the most senior available engineer to coordinate communication and response.

The incident response steps are: (1) Detect and acknowledge, (2) Assess severity and open incident channel, (3) Identify contributing factors using available monitoring dashboards in Datadog, (4) Implement mitigation (rollback, feature flag disable, traffic rerouting), (5) Confirm recovery via synthetic monitoring passing for 5 consecutive minutes, (6) Notify client account manager if client-facing impact exceeded 5 minutes, (7) Post preliminary incident summary in the incident channel within 1 hour of resolution. A full post-mortem must be completed within 5 business days for all SEV1 and SEV2 incidents using our blameless post-mortem template at internal.meridian.dev/postmortem-template. Post-mortems are reviewed in the monthly Engineering All-Hands meeting on the third Tuesday of each month.

title: On-Call Rotation Procedures
content:
Meridian Consulting maintains a 24/7 on-call rotation for production systems across all active client engagements. The on-call schedule is managed in PagerDuty and set quarterly by engineering managers in collaboration with their teams. The current rotation includes 8 eligible engineers (L3 and above) organized in a weekly rotation. Each engineer is on-call as primary for one week, with a secondary backup from the same project team. On-call weeks begin on Monday at 9:00 AM EST and end the following Monday at 8:59 AM EST. Engineers on primary on-call are expected to respond to PagerDuty alerts within 10 minutes and must be reachable by phone. Secondary on-call must be reachable within 20 minutes. Engineers must not schedule travel, medical procedures, or other commitments that would impair response capability during their on-call week without arranging a covered swap at least 72 hours in advance through the #on-call-swaps Slack channel.

On-call engineers receive a $200 on-call stipend per week regardless of incident volume, plus $50 per SEV1 incident response and $25 per SEV2 response occurring outside of 9 AM–6 PM EST on weekdays. These payments are processed through payroll in the following pay cycle and are itemized on pay stubs. After a major incident (SEV1), the on-call engineer may take a compensatory half-day within five business days. On-call handoffs occur via a written summary in the #on-call-log Slack channel, covering open incidents, recent deployments, known instability, and any temporary mitigations in place. New engineers must shadow on-call for two full rotations and participate in at least one simulated incident drill before being added to the active rotation. The current Q1 2025 schedule was published January 2, 2025 by engineering manager Sofia Reyes.

title: Tech Debt Management Policy
content:
Meridian Consulting formally recognizes technical debt as a business risk requiring structured management rather than ad-hoc resolution. All identified technical debt items must be logged in Linear with the label `tech-debt` and a severity classification: Critical (actively blocking development or posing security risk), High (slowing velocity by more than 20% for the affected team), Medium (creates friction but workarounds exist), and Low (cosmetic or minor inefficiency). Debt items must include an estimated remediation effort in story points, the engineer who identified it, the date identified, and the business impact description. Debt items are reviewed monthly by the tech lead and engineering manager for each project.

Each two-week sprint must allocate a minimum of 15% of total sprint capacity to technical debt remediation, calculated as 15% of the team's average velocity. For projects with a Critical debt item, capacity allocation increases to 25% until the critical item is resolved. Engineering managers report on aggregate debt levels in the monthly Engineering All-Hands using a debt scorecard that tracks total open debt points, debt resolved in the past month, and the debt-to-feature ratio. When onboarding a new client engagement that involves inheriting an existing codebase, a dedicated tech debt audit is conducted in the first two weeks, producing a Debt Register delivered to the client as part of the engagement kickoff. Client-facing debt remediation work is scoped and billed at the standard consulting rate unless it falls within a dedicated maintenance retainer. The engineering manager, currently Sofia Reyes, has final authority on debt prioritization disputes.

title: Monitoring and Alerting Procedures
content:
All production services at Meridian Consulting are monitored using Datadog as the primary observability platform. Each service must emit the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation. Service-level dashboards are created from the Meridian Datadog dashboard template (`meridian-svc-dashboard-v3`) at the time of the service's first production deployment. P50, P95, and P99 latency must be tracked per endpoint. Error rates are tracked as a percentage of total requests, and a rate above 1% triggers a SEV3 alert by default. CPU and memory saturation alerts fire at 80% sustained over 5 minutes. Custom business metrics, such as order processing rates or job queue depth, must also be instrumented and included in client-facing SLA dashboards where applicable.

Alerts are routed through PagerDuty with policies defined per project. Non-urgent alerts (SEV3, SEV4) are routed to the #alerts-<project-name> Slack channel and do not page anyone outside business hours. SEV1 and SEV2 alerts always page the on-call engineer regardless of time. Alert fatigue is actively managed: any alert that fires more than 10 times in a 7-day period without resulting in a confirmed incident must be tuned or suppressed within 5 business days. The DevOps lead, Marcus Chen, conducts a monthly alert quality review across all projects and issues a report to engineering managers. Synthetic monitoring using Datadog Synthetics runs every 5 minutes against all public-facing endpoints, covering the primary user journeys. Uptime data from synthetics is used to calculate SLA compliance reported to clients monthly.

title: Client Engagement Technical Onboarding
content:
When a new client engagement is confirmed, the assigned lead engineer must complete a Technical Onboarding Checklist within the first 5 business days of the project start date. The checklist, maintained at internal.meridian.dev/client-tech-onboarding, includes: provisioning a dedicated AWS sub-account under the Meridian AWS Organization, creating the project's GitHub organization (format: `meridian-<clientname>`), setting up the project in Linear with standard labels and milestone structure, configuring Datadog for the project, establishing a dedicated Slack Connect channel with the client, creating a project space in the internal knowledge base, and scheduling recurring ceremonies with the client. All client systems accessed by Meridian engineers require documented access credentials stored in the project's dedicated AWS Secrets Manager namespace, never in personal vaults or shared documents.

A kickoff technical assessment is conducted in the first two weeks, covering the client's existing infrastructure, data flows, third-party integrations, and known technical constraints. The output is a Technical Assessment Report (TAR) delivered to the client and stored in the project's Confluence space. This report informs sprint planning for the first quarter of the engagement. Engineers working on client systems must use Meridian-managed devices and VPN at all times; personal device access is prohibited. Offboarding at engagement end requires a reverse checklist: transferring ownership of all provisioned resources, revoking all Meridian access credentials, delivering a final Architecture Documentation Package, and completing a 60-minute knowledge transfer session with the client's technical team. Offboarding must be completed within 10 business days of the contract end date as specified in the Master Services Agreement.

title: Data Handling and Privacy Policy
content:
Meridian Consulting engineers are required to handle all client and end-user data in compliance with applicable regulations including GDPR, CCPA, and HIPAA where relevant to specific client engagements. Data classification is mandatory before any new data store or data flow is implemented. Classifications are: Public (no restrictions), Internal (Meridian staff only), Confidential (need-to-know basis within the engagement team), and Restricted (PII, PHI, PCI data requiring encryption at rest and in transit, access logging, and explicit DPA coverage). Engineers must never use production data in development or testing environments; synthetic data generation tools including Faker and Mimesis are approved and documented at internal.meridian.dev/test-data-generation.

All Restricted data at rest must use AES-256 encryption via AWS KMS with per-client CMKs. Data in transit must use TLS 1.2 minimum, with TLS 1.3 required for new systems launched after January 1, 2025. Data retention schedules must be implemented technically, not just documented; automated deletion jobs must be in place for data exceeding the agreed retention period. Access to Restricted data is logged to CloudTrail and must be justifiable upon audit. Engineers must complete GDPR Fundamentals training within 30 days of hire and annually thereafter. Any suspected data breach or unauthorized access must be reported immediately to the security champion and the engineering manager, who notifies the DPO (currently Legal Counsel, James Okafor) within 1 hour. Clients must be notified within 72 hours of confirmed breach per GDPR Article 33 obligations, with notification drafted by the DPO in coordination with the account manager.

title: Engineer Performance Review Criteria
content:
Meridian Consulting conducts formal engineer performance reviews biannually: mid-year assessments in July and annual reviews in January. Reviews use a structured scorecard with five weighted dimensions: (1) Technical Execution (35%) — code quality metrics from pull request data, adherence to standards, test coverage contributions, and absence of recurring defects; (2) Collaboration and Communication (20%) — PR review participation rate (target: ≥4 reviews per sprint), responsiveness in Slack, and 360-degree peer feedback scores averaged from at least 3 peers; (3) Delivery and Ownership (20%) — sprint velocity consistency, story point completion rate vs. commitment, incident response behavior, and on-call performance; (4) Growth and Learning (15%) — certifications completed, Lunch & Learn presentations delivered, ADR contributions, and engagement with the mentorship program; and (5) Client and Business Impact (10%) — qualitative assessment from the account manager and, where applicable, direct client feedback.

Engineers are scored on a 1–5 scale per dimension, producing a weighted composite score. Scores of 4.0 or above qualify an engineer for promotion consideration. Scores below 2.5 trigger a Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) initiated by the engineering manager in consultation with HR, with 60-day measurable targets. Compensation increases are tied to composite score bands: 4.5–5.0 (8–10% increase), 4.0–4.4 (5–7%), 3.0–3.9 (2–4%), below 3.0 (no increase, PIP). Promotion decisions from L2 to L3 and L3 to L4 require two consecutive review cycles at 4.0+ and sign-off from both the engineering manager and the CTO. All review scores and compensation details are strictly confidential, accessible only to the engineer, their manager, HR, and the CTO.

title: Project Financial and Billing Rates
content:
Meridian Consulting's engineering billing rates are tiered by engineer level and are subject to the terms of each client's Master Services Agreement. Standard 2025 rates are as follows: L1 Engineer (0–2 years experience): $145/hour; L2 Engineer (2–4 years): $175/hour; L3 Senior Engineer (4–7 years): $215/hour; L4 Staff Engineer (7–12 years): $260/hour; L5 Principal Engineer (12+ years): $310/hour. A 15% premium applies to engagements requiring HIPAA compliance or FedRAMP-aligned security posture due to the additional overhead of compliance documentation and security review cycles. Blended team rates are negotiated for fixed-price engagements and are calculated using the projected team composition, with a 20% margin applied above blended cost. DevOps and infrastructure work billed at a flat rate of $195/hour regardless of engineer level for standardization.

Project financial health is reviewed weekly by engineering managers and account managers using the Engagement Financial Dashboard in Salesforce. Each project has a budgeted burn rate; alerts are triggered when actual burn exceeds budgeted burn by more than 10% for two consecutive weeks. Over-budget situations must be escalated to the VP of Delivery within 24 hours with a remediation plan. Change orders are required for any scope expansion exceeding 15% of the original SOW hours. Engineers must log time daily in Harvest by 6:00 PM in their local timezone, tagged by project code, and must never log time to a project code they are not assigned to. Timesheet discrepancies identified during monthly reconciliation are reviewed by the CFO and can result in disciplinary action. Rate information and financial data are strictly confidential and must not be shared with engineers, clients, or external parties without VP of Delivery approval.

title: Vendor and Contractor Agreements
content:
Meridian Consulting periodically engages specialist contractors and third-party vendors to supplement engineering capacity or provide niche expertise. All contractor engagements are governed by a Master Contractor Agreement reviewed and signed by the COO and the contractor before any work begins. Current active contractor engagements as of January 2025 include: Apex Security Partners LLC (penetration testing, $18,500 per annual engagement, renewed November 2024), DataViz Studio (frontend UX/data visualization specialist, contracted at $185/hour, current SOW for Hartwell Financial engagement not to exceed 320 hours), and CloudArch Consulting (AWS architecture review, $22,000 fixed fee, SOW signed December 10, 2024 for delivery by February 28, 2025). All contractors must sign Meridian's standard NDA and IP Assignment Agreement before receiving any access to internal systems or client information.

Contractor access is provisioned through a separate contractor IAM group in AWS with time-boxed credentials that auto-expire at contract end. GitHub access is granted to a dedicated `contractors` team with read/write permissions limited to the specific repositories required. Contractors may not approve pull requests; only full-time Meridian engineers may grant PR approvals. Contractor timesheets are submitted weekly to the assigned engineering manager for approval, then forwarded to Finance for invoice processing with NET-30 payment terms. Any contractor performance issues must be escalated to the COO within 2 business days. Contractor rate information, contract terms, and vendor financial details are strictly confidential and accessible only to the engineering manager, the COO, the CFO, and the legal team. Sharing this information with other engineers or clients without COO approval is a policy violation subject to disciplinary action.

title: Expense Report Submission Policy
content:
All employees are required to submit expense reports within 30 days of incurring a business expense. Reports must be submitted through the company's expense management platform, Concur, and must include original itemized receipts for any expense exceeding $25. Expenses submitted after the 30-day window will require written approval from the employee's direct manager and the CFO, Sarah Brennan, before reimbursement can be processed. Reports submitted by the 15th of each month will be reimbursed by the last business day of that same month; reports submitted after the 15th will be processed in the following month's reimbursement cycle.

Expenses must be categorized using the firm's standard chart of accounts, which includes categories such as Client Entertainment (GL 6120), Business Travel (GL 6200), Office Supplies (GL 6050), and Professional Development (GL 6310). Any single expense exceeding $500 requires a secondary approval from the department head prior to submission. Alcohol purchases are only reimbursable when accompanied by a client and must not exceed 20% of the total meal bill. Employees are reminded that personal expenses, commuting costs, and any purchases unrelated to firm business are strictly ineligible for reimbursement. Questions about eligible expense categories should be directed to the Accounting team at accounting@firmname.com.

title: Client Invoice Issuance Procedures
content:
Invoices to clients are generated by the Accounting department on the first and fifteenth of each month, corresponding to completed project milestones or time-and-materials billing cycles. Project managers are responsible for submitting a billing summary to accounting@firmname.com no later than three business days before each invoice date, using the standardized Billing Summary Form (Form AC-102). The billing summary must include the client name, project code, billing period, hours by staff member, and any approved out-of-pocket expenses to be passed through. Invoices are issued via QuickBooks Online and delivered to the client's designated billing contact by email in PDF format.

Standard payment terms are Net 30 from the invoice date unless otherwise specified in the client's Master Services Agreement. Invoices must reference the client's purchase order number when one has been provided. If a client disputes any line item, the project manager must respond to the dispute in writing within five business days, and the Accounting team will place a hold on the disputed amount while the remainder of the invoice remains due. Invoices unpaid after 45 days will trigger an automated reminder from QuickBooks, followed by a personal outreach from Accounts Receivable Specialist, Tom Nakamura. Persistent non-payment beyond 90 days will be escalated to senior leadership for potential engagement of collections counsel.

title: Annual Budget Template Guidelines
content:
Each department head must complete and submit their annual budget using the standardized Budget Template (File: FY_Budget_Template_v4.xlsx) available on the company SharePoint portal under Finance > Budget Resources. Budget submissions for the upcoming fiscal year are due to the Accounting department by October 15th, allowing sufficient time for consolidation and review before the November 1st board presentation. The template includes tabs for Personnel Costs, Operating Expenses, Capital Expenditures, and Revenue Projections. Department heads should base their projections on actual prior-year spending with documented assumptions for any variance greater than 10%.

Personnel costs must reflect current salary data pulled from HR as of September 1st, plus an assumed merit increase pool of 3.5% for the upcoming year, unless otherwise communicated by the CEO. Headcount changes, including planned new hires or attrition, must be reflected on the FTE Planning tab with expected start or departure dates. All capital expenditure requests exceeding $5,000 must be accompanied by a Capital Expenditure Justification Form (Form AC-205) submitted simultaneously with the budget. Once submitted, the CFO's office will schedule individual budget review meetings with each department head between October 20th and October 28th. Revisions requested following those meetings must be resubmitted within two business days. Failure to meet submission deadlines may result in the prior year's budget being used as a placeholder, which could limit discretionary spending approvals in Q1.

title: Internal Audit Procedures Overview
content:
The firm conducts internal audits on a quarterly basis to ensure compliance with financial policies, accuracy of reported figures, and adherence to internal controls. Audits are coordinated by the Controller, Linda Forsythe, and may involve spot checks of expense reports, vendor payments, payroll records, and client billings. Employees selected for audit review will be notified at least two business days in advance and are expected to provide all requested documentation within three business days of the request. The firm also conducts an annual comprehensive audit in January covering the prior fiscal year, the results of which are shared with the executive leadership team and the board of directors.

All audit findings are categorized as Low, Medium, or High risk. High-risk findings require a corrective action plan to be submitted to the CFO within 10 business days of the audit report being issued. Medium-risk findings must be addressed within 30 days, and Low-risk findings must be resolved within 90 days. Repeat findings from prior audits are automatically elevated one risk category. The firm retains all audit documentation for a minimum of seven years in accordance with IRS recordkeeping requirements. Employees are reminded that cooperation with audit requests is a condition of employment, and any obstruction or falsification of records will be subject to immediate disciplinary action up to and including termination.

title: Financial Reporting Standards Guide
content:
The firm prepares financial reports in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) and follows a standardized reporting calendar. Monthly financial statements, including the Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Statement of Cash Flows, are finalized by the 10th business day following month-end and distributed to department heads and senior leadership via a secure SharePoint folder. Quarterly financial packages, which include additional variance analysis and rolling 12-month trend data, are prepared by the 15th business day following quarter-end. The annual financial statements are completed by February 28th of the following year.

All financial reports must use the firm's standard chart of accounts and be prepared using QuickBooks Online as the system of record. Any manual journal entries must include a detailed description of the business purpose, supporting documentation, and approval from the Controller before posting. Intercompany transactions, if applicable, must be eliminated in consolidated reports. Department heads who receive monthly reports are expected to review their cost center actuals against budget and provide written commentary on variances exceeding $2,500 or 15% of budget, whichever is lower, no later than the 12th business day of the following month. These variance explanations are incorporated into the executive financial package prepared by the CFO's office.

title: Vendor Payment Process Documentation
content:
All vendor invoices must be submitted to the Accounts Payable inbox at ap@firmname.com or delivered in physical form to the Accounting department no later than the 5th of the month to be included in that month's payment run. Invoices must reference an approved Purchase Order (PO) number; invoices received without a valid PO will be returned to the vendor and will not be processed until a PO is obtained. The Accounts Payable team, currently managed by Diana Chu, performs a three-way match — comparing the vendor invoice against the original PO and the receiving confirmation — before any payment is approved. Discrepancies identified during the three-way match will be escalated to the originating department for resolution.

The firm processes vendor payments twice monthly: on the 10th and 25th of each month via ACH transfer. Checks are issued only in exceptional circumstances and require written justification and CFO approval. Standard payment terms are Net 30 unless a vendor contract specifies otherwise or an early payment discount of at least 2% has been negotiated. Urgent payment requests outside the standard payment schedule require approval from the Controller and will incur an administrative fee of $15 charged to the requesting department's cost center. Vendor banking information changes must be verified by phone call to a known vendor contact before being updated in QuickBooks to prevent fraud. The firm maintains a preferred vendor list, and any new vendor onboarding requires completion of a W-9 form and vendor due diligence review.

title: Employee Travel Reimbursement Rules
content:
Business travel must be approved in advance by the employee's direct manager for any trip with estimated costs exceeding $250. For domestic air travel, employees must book at least 14 days in advance when possible to secure reasonable fares and are expected to select economy class unless the flight duration exceeds six hours, in which case premium economy is permitted with manager approval. All travel bookings should be made through the firm's preferred travel portal, TravelPerk, to ensure compliance with negotiated rates. Hotel stays are reimbursable up to $225 per night in standard domestic markets and up to $300 per night in designated high-cost cities including New York, San Francisco, Boston, and Washington D.C.

Meal per diems follow the GSA federal per diem rates for the destination city, updated annually each October 1st. Employees should retain itemized receipts for all meal expenses; per diem is the maximum reimbursable amount and not a guaranteed flat payment — actual lower costs will be reimbursed at actual cost. Ground transportation should use the most economical option; personal vehicle use is reimbursed at the current IRS standard mileage rate, which is $0.67 per mile for 2024. Rental cars require advance approval and must include the collision damage waiver purchased through the rental agency, as the firm's corporate card provides secondary coverage only. Employees traveling internationally must notify both their manager and the HR department at least two weeks in advance and must consult the CFO's office regarding any foreign currency expense documentation requirements.

title: Procurement Policy and Procedures
content:
All procurement of goods and services on behalf of the firm must follow the tiered approval process outlined in this policy. Purchases under $500 may be approved by the employee's direct manager and charged to the appropriate cost center using a company credit card or petty cash. Purchases between $500 and $5,000 require a completed Purchase Requisition Form (Form AC-301) submitted through the SharePoint procurement portal and approved by the department head. Purchases between $5,001 and $25,000 additionally require approval from the CFO and must be accompanied by at least two competitive quotes. Purchases exceeding $25,000 require a formal RFP process coordinated by the Operations team and final approval from the CEO.

All software subscriptions and SaaS tools, regardless of cost, must be reviewed and approved by both IT and the CFO's office before purchase to prevent unauthorized data sharing and ensure license optimization across the firm. Recurring contracts with vendors must be reviewed annually by the department head and the Accounting team to confirm continued value and competitive pricing. Purchase Orders are issued by the Accounting department for all approved purchases above $500 and must be referenced on all subsequent vendor invoices. Employees are prohibited from splitting purchases across multiple smaller transactions to circumvent approval thresholds; violations will be treated as a policy breach and may result in disciplinary action. The preferred vendor list is maintained by Diana Chu and should be the first point of reference when sourcing new goods or services.

title: Tax Compliance Procedures Manual
content:
The firm is subject to federal, state, and local tax obligations and relies on its external CPA firm, Hartwell & Associates, for preparation of annual business tax returns. The Controller, Linda Forsythe, serves as the primary liaison between the firm and Hartwell & Associates and is responsible for ensuring timely delivery of all requested financial data. The firm's fiscal year aligns with the calendar year, and the federal tax return (Form 1120-S, as the firm is structured as an S-Corporation) must be filed by March 15th, with an extension available through September 15th if necessary. The Accounting team must deliver final trial balance and supporting schedules to Hartwell & Associates by February 15th each year.

Quarterly estimated tax payments are calculated by Hartwell & Associates and must be funded and remitted by the 15th of April, June, September, and January. Sales tax applicability is reviewed on a state-by-state basis given the firm's multi-state consulting engagements; the Controller reviews nexus status annually each January and coordinates with Hartwell & Associates to register in new states as required. All employees who receive non-salary compensation, including bonuses, commissions, or reimbursements of non-deductible expenses, must receive accurate W-2 reporting. 1099-NEC forms must be issued to all eligible independent contractors and vendors receiving $600 or more in a calendar year; a master 1099 list is compiled by the Accounting team every December with final forms issued by January 31st.

title: Accounts Receivable Collections Process
content:
The Accounts Receivable process begins the moment a client invoice is issued and is actively managed by Tom Nakamura, AR Specialist. Upon issuance, invoices are logged in QuickBooks Online and monitored against their due dates on a weekly basis. An automated payment reminder is sent to the client's billing contact seven days before the invoice due date. If payment is not received by the due date, a second reminder is sent on Day 1 post-due, followed by a personal email from Tom Nakamura on Day 10. If no payment or response has been received by Day 20 past due, the project manager is looped in to assist with client outreach, as the relationship owner.

Invoices 45 days past due are escalated to the CFO, Sarah Brennan, who reviews the account and may elect to pause active project work pending payment, in coordination with the project manager. Invoices 90 days past due are reviewed for potential write-off or referral to the firm's collections attorney, Morrison Legal Group. Before any write-off is recorded, the CFO must approve the write-off amount, and the Controller must post the appropriate journal entry to the Bad Debt Expense account (GL 7100). The firm targets a Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) of no more than 42 days and reviews DSO performance monthly as part of the financial reporting package. Any client with a history of late payment may be required to pay a deposit of 25% of estimated project fees before new work commences.

title: Accounts Payable Workflow Documentation
content:
The Accounts Payable workflow begins upon receipt of a vendor invoice at ap@firmname.com or physical delivery to Diana Chu's desk. Upon receipt, Diana logs the invoice in the AP tracking spreadsheet (located on SharePoint under Finance > AP Management) within one business day, noting the vendor name, invoice number, invoice date, amount, GL account, and cost center. Invoices are then matched against open Purchase Orders in QuickBooks. Invoices with a valid PO match and no discrepancy are coded and queued for the next payment run. Invoices requiring clarification are placed in a pending status and the originating department is notified via email with a response deadline of three business days.

Payment batches are compiled on the 8th and 23rd of each month, reviewed by the Controller, and approved by the CFO before ACH files are transmitted to First National Bank by 2:00 PM on the payment dates of the 10th and 25th. Diana maintains a running AP aging report that is distributed to the Controller and CFO every Monday morning. Vendor statements are reconciled monthly to ensure no invoices have been missed. Any credit memos received from vendors must be applied to outstanding balances before the next payment run and must be documented in QuickBooks with a reference to the original invoice. AP records, including invoices, POs, and payment confirmations, are retained in the secure SharePoint AP archive folder for a minimum of seven years per the firm's document retention policy.

title: Month-End Close Checklist Procedures
content:
The month-end close process must be completed by the 8th business day following the last day of each month. The Controller, Linda Forsythe, owns the master close checklist (File: Month_End_Close_Checklist_FY2024.xlsx) and is responsible for assigning tasks to Accounting team members with specific deadlines. Key tasks in the close process include: reconciling all bank accounts by Business Day 3; posting all accruals and prepaid amortization entries by Business Day 4; reconciling intercompany accounts and eliminating entries by Business Day 5; completing fixed asset depreciation runs in QuickBooks by Business Day 5; reviewing and posting payroll journal entries by Business Day 3 following receipt of the payroll register from ADP; and ensuring all vendor invoices received through the last day of the month are recorded or accrued.

Revenue recognition entries must be reviewed by the Controller to ensure compliance with the firm's revenue recognition policy and ASC 606 guidance. All journal entries must include a description, supporting documentation attached in QuickBooks, and must be approved by the Controller before the books are locked. Books are locked on Business Day 9, after which no entries may be posted to the prior month without a formal prior-period adjustment request approved by the CFO. Following the lock, financial statements are generated, reviewed, and distributed to department heads by Business Day 10. A close debrief is held the following week to discuss any items that required late adjustments and to identify process improvements for the subsequent month.

title: Client Billing and Invoicing Guidelines
content:
Client billing rates are established in each client's fully executed Statement of Work (SOW) or Master Services Agreement (MSA) and must be strictly adhered to when preparing invoices. The Accounting team cross-references billing rates against the signed contract on file before finalizing each invoice cycle. Time-and-materials invoices must reflect actual hours worked as recorded in Harvest, the firm's time-tracking system, and may not include estimated or rounded hours. Fixed-fee invoices must align with milestone completion confirmations provided by the project manager in writing before the invoice is issued. Billing disputes resolved in the client's favor must be processed as a credit memo and applied to the subsequent invoice or refunded within 15 days, per the client's preference.

Internally, the firm distinguishes between billable and non-billable time. Billable utilization targets are 75% for Senior Consultants, 80% for Consultants, and 65% for Managing Consultants, reflecting client-facing versus internal responsibilities. The billing team monitors utilization rates monthly and flags projects where billed hours exceed the SOW budget by more than 10%, which triggers a project manager review and potential client change order discussion. All change orders must be executed in writing before additional fees are invoiced. The Accounting team maintains a billing rate card (File: Rate_Card_Internal_FY2024.xlsx) on SharePoint that is updated each January 1st and reflects standard, discounted, and blended rates. This file is restricted to Accounting staff and senior leadership and must not be shared with clients or external parties.

title: Timesheet Submission and Approval Policy
content:
All employees are required to submit timesheets in Harvest on a weekly basis, with timesheets for each week due by 11:59 PM on Sunday. Timesheets must reflect actual time worked, categorized by project code and task type (e.g., Client Delivery, Business Development, Internal Administration, Training). Minimum entry increments are 15 minutes, and entries must include a brief description of the work performed to support client billing and project reporting. Employees are not permitted to pre-fill or bulk-enter time at the end of a pay period; timesheets showing large blocks of identical daily entries without supporting detail may be flagged for review by the project manager.

Managers are responsible for reviewing and approving their direct reports' timesheets in Harvest by 9:00 AM on Monday of the following week. Unapproved timesheets prevent the Accounting team from generating accurate billing summaries and will result in those project hours being excluded from the current billing cycle. Employees who miss the Sunday submission deadline must notify their manager and submit by Monday at noon, noting the late submission reason in the Harvest comments field. Chronic late submission — defined as three or more occurrences within a rolling 90-day period — will be noted in the employee's performance record. The Payroll team uses Harvest data to validate exempt employee project allocation and to process overtime calculations for any non-exempt staff; accurate timesheet data is therefore critical not only for billing but for payroll compliance.

title: Petty Cash Fund Management Procedures
content:
The firm maintains a petty cash fund of $500, held in a locked cash box in the Accounting office and managed by Diana Chu as petty cash custodian. Petty cash is intended for minor, immediate business expenses that are impractical to process through the standard purchasing workflow, such as small office supply purchases, postage, or incidental client hospitality items under $50. Each disbursement requires the employee to complete a Petty Cash Voucher (Form AC-410) listing the date, amount, business purpose, GL account, and cost center, and to attach a receipt. Disbursements without a receipt will only be approved for amounts under $10, and the employee must provide a written explanation.

When the fund balance falls below $100, Diana submits a replenishment request to the Controller with a reconciliation summary showing all disbursements since the last replenishment. The Controller reviews and approves the reconciliation, and a check or ACH transfer equal to the total disbursements is issued to restore the fund to $500. The petty cash fund is subject to surprise counts by the Controller at least once per quarter; the count must reconcile to within $5 of the expected balance (cash plus outstanding vouchers). Any discrepancy exceeding $5 must be investigated and documented within 24 hours. If a discrepancy cannot be explained, it is written off to the Miscellaneous Expense account (GL 6990) with CFO notification. The fund is fully counted and reconciled as part of the year-end close process each December 31st.

title: Financial Approval Authority Thresholds
content:
The firm operates under a formal Delegation of Authority (DOA) matrix that defines financial approval rights by role and transaction type. For operational expenditures, individual contributors may approve purchases up to $500 using corporate card or petty cash with manager ratification. Managers and Senior Managers may approve expenditures up to $2,500. Directors and Vice Presidents hold approval authority up to $10,000. The CFO, Sarah Brennan, approves expenditures from $10,001 to $50,000. Transactions exceeding $50,000 require joint approval from the CFO and CEO. All approvals must be documented in writing, either through the SharePoint procurement portal, email confirmation, or a signed Purchase Requisition Form, before a commitment is made to a vendor.

For contract execution, only the CFO and CEO are authorized to sign agreements on behalf of the firm; no other employee may execute a legally binding contract regardless of dollar value without explicit written delegation. Payroll changes — including new hires, terminations, salary adjustments, and bonuses — require approval from the department head and CFO before submission to ADP. Journal entries affecting the Balance Sheet must be approved by the Controller; journal entries in excess of $25,000 require secondary approval from the CFO. Wire transfers require dual authorization: initiation by the Controller and release approval by the CFO, with all wire documentation retained for audit purposes. The DOA matrix is reviewed annually each January and updated as needed to reflect organizational changes.

title: Cost Center Assignment and Usage Guidelines
content:
The firm's chart of accounts includes 12 active cost centers, each assigned to a specific department or function: CC-100 (Executive Leadership), CC-110 (Finance & Accounting), CC-120 (Human Resources), CC-130 (Information Technology), CC-140 (Marketing), CC-150 (Operations), CC-200 (Consulting – East Region), CC-210 (Consulting – West Region), CC-220 (Consulting – Central Region), CC-300 (Business Development), CC-400 (Facilities), and CC-500 (Unallocated/Overhead). All expenses must be coded to the most appropriate cost center at the time of entry; bulk allocation or frequent reassignment between cost centers is not permitted without Controller approval. Department heads receive monthly cost center reports and are responsible for monitoring their cost center's spending against budget.

New cost centers may be requested by department heads by submitting a Cost Center Request Form (Form AC-505) to the Controller for review and CFO approval. Cost centers that show no activity for six consecutive months are subject to inactivation, after which any charges attempted against them will require reactivation. Project-specific cost centers may be established for engagements exceeding $500,000 in projected revenue to enable precise project-level profitability tracking; these are created upon request from the project manager and approved by the Controller. Employees should reference the most current Cost Center Reference Guide (File: Cost_Center_Reference_FY2024.pdf) on SharePoint before coding any expense. Incorrect cost center coding discovered during review must be corrected via journal entry within the same accounting period, with a notation referencing the original transaction.

title: Revenue Recognition Policy Statement
content:
The firm recognizes revenue in accordance with ASC 606, Revenue from Contracts with Customers. Under this standard, revenue is recognized when, or as, performance obligations are satisfied. The firm's contracts with clients fall into two primary categories: (1) time-and-materials engagements, under which revenue is recognized as services are rendered, measured by hours worked multiplied by the applicable billing rate; and (2) fixed-fee engagements, under which revenue is recognized over time using the input method, based on costs incurred to date relative to total estimated costs. The Controller is responsible for reviewing the classification of each new contract within five business days of execution and documenting the revenue recognition method in the contract register maintained in SharePoint.

For fixed-fee contracts, project managers must provide an updated estimate-to-complete (ETC) to the Accounting team by the 25th of each month. Changes in ETC that result in a projected loss must be recognized immediately in the current period as a loss accrual, with the journal entry approved by the CFO. Variable consideration, such as performance bonuses or success fees included in client contracts, is recognized only to the extent it is probable that a significant reversal will not occur, consistent with ASC 606-10-32. Contract modifications, including change orders, are evaluated by the Controller to determine whether they represent a new contract or a modification of the existing contract, with the appropriate accounting treatment documented and on file. All revenue recognition judgments involving subjectivity above $50,000 must be reviewed by the external auditors at Hartwell & Associates as part of the quarterly review process. This document contains forward-looking financial estimates and contract structure details that are strictly confidential.

title: Corporate Credit Card Usage Policy
content:
Corporate credit cards are issued through the firm's commercial account with Chase Bank and are available to employees at the Manager level and above, as well as to any employee with demonstrated business need approved by the CFO. Current cardholders are listed in the confidential cardholder registry maintained by Diana Chu. Each card carries an individual monthly spending limit assigned based on the employee's role and expected business needs: Managers are assigned a $2,000 monthly limit; Directors and VPs are assigned a $5,000 monthly limit; the CFO and CEO hold cards with a $15,000 monthly limit. Requests to temporarily increase a card limit must be submitted to Diana Chu with manager and CFO approval and will be adjusted for the specific billing cycle only.

Cardholders are required to reconcile their monthly statement in Concur by the 5th of the following month, attaching receipts and coding each transaction to the appropriate GL account and cost center. Unreconciled transactions after the 10th will result in the card being temporarily suspended. Personal charges on the corporate card are strictly prohibited; any personal charge identified during reconciliation review must be repaid by the employee via check to the firm within five business days. Repeated personal charges will result in permanent card revocation and potential disciplinary action. Lost or stolen cards must be reported to Diana Chu and Chase Bank within one hour of discovery. The complete cardholder list, individual credit limits, and card numbers are maintained as confidential information and are not to be shared outside of the Accounting team and senior leadership.

title: Charitable Donation Matching Program Procedures
content:
The firm offers a charitable donation matching program to all full-time employees with a minimum of six months of tenure. The firm will match eligible employee donations at a 1:1 ratio up to $1,000 per employee per calendar year. Total firm-wide matching budget for FY2024 is capped at $30,000, as approved by the board of directors in November 2023. Eligible organizations must be U.S.-based 501(c)(3) registered charities in good standing; political organizations, religious organizations providing exclusively sectarian programming, and private foundations are not eligible. Employees submit matching requests through the HR portal (BambooHR) by attaching a donation receipt and confirming the organization's EIN and 501(c)(3) status.

The HR team validates eligibility and routes approved requests to the Accounting department for payment processing. Matching donations are disbursed quarterly: in April, July, October, and January, covering requests received in the prior quarter. Payments are made by check directly to the charitable organization and recorded in QuickBooks under GL 7500 (Charitable Contributions) against Cost Center CC-100. The firm issues a consolidated year-end summary of all matching payments to the CFO and CEO for board reporting purposes. This information is kept confidential as the aggregate donation figures, individual employee participation data, and the firm's total charitable giving budget are considered sensitive for competitive and tax planning reasons. Any external communication about the matching program must be reviewed and approved by the CFO and CEO before release.

title: Annual PTO Policy Overview
content:
Meridian Consulting Group offers all full-time employees a competitive paid time off (PTO) policy designed to support work-life balance. Employees accrue PTO based on years of service: 0–2 years earn 15 days per year, 3–5 years earn 18 days per year, and 6+ years earn 22 days per year. PTO accrues at the beginning of each calendar quarter in equal installments. Part-time employees working at least 20 hours per week accrue PTO on a prorated basis. In addition to standard PTO, all employees receive 11 paid company holidays annually, including New Year's Day, Memorial Day, Independence Day, Labor Day, Thanksgiving Day, the Friday after Thanksgiving, and Christmas Day, plus four floating holidays employees may schedule at their discretion.

Employees must submit PTO requests through the BambooHR portal at least 5 business days in advance for absences of 3 or more consecutive days, and at least 24 hours in advance for single-day requests, subject to manager approval. Unused PTO may be carried over up to a maximum of 10 days into the following calendar year; any balance exceeding 10 days is forfeited on January 1. Upon separation from the company, accrued and unused PTO will be paid out in the final paycheck in accordance with applicable state law. Employees experiencing a medical emergency may request an early draw of up to 5 PTO days against the next quarter's accrual with written approval from HR Director Sandra Kowalski.

title: New Employee Onboarding Checklist
content:
Welcome to Meridian Consulting Group! This onboarding checklist is designed to ensure every new hire has a smooth and productive first 30 days. During your first day, you will complete I-9 and W-4 forms with HR Coordinator Jamie Lin, receive your company laptop and access credentials from IT Specialist Derek Okonkwo, attend a 90-minute company orientation session covering our mission, values, and organizational structure, and be introduced to your assigned onboarding buddy. By the end of your first week, you should have completed all mandatory compliance training modules in Meridian's learning management system (TalentLMS), including Data Privacy, Anti-Harassment, and Information Security Awareness. You will also schedule one-on-one meetings with each member of your immediate team.

During weeks two through four, new employees are expected to shadow at least two active client engagements, review the Meridian Client Engagement Handbook, complete setup of your Salesforce CRM profile and Slack workspace, and attend the bi-weekly New Hire Lunch hosted by Managing Partner Elena Torres on the first and third Friday of each month. By day 30, your direct manager will conduct a 30-Day Check-In meeting to discuss initial observations, address any questions, and set 60- and 90-day performance goals. All checklist items must be verified and signed off in BambooHR before the 30-day mark. If any item is incomplete, HR should be notified immediately to reschedule or make alternative arrangements. Questions about the onboarding process can be directed to Jamie Lin at jlin@meridianconsulting.com.

title: Employee Benefits Guide 2024
content:
Meridian Consulting Group provides a comprehensive benefits package to all full-time employees working 30 or more hours per week. Health insurance is offered through BlueCross BlueShield with three plan tiers: the Core PPO plan (employee premium of $85/month), the Enhanced PPO plan ($145/month), and the High-Deductible Health Plan with HSA ($42/month with a company HSA contribution of $600/year for individual coverage and $1,200/year for family coverage). Dental coverage is provided through Delta Dental, with individual premiums at $18/month and family at $54/month. Vision insurance is through VSP, costing employees $8/month for individual and $22/month for family coverage. Meridian covers 80% of all health, dental, and vision premiums for employee-only enrollment and 65% for dependent coverage. Benefits become effective on the first day of the month following 30 days of employment.

Additional benefits include a 401(k) retirement plan administered by Fidelity, with Meridian matching 100% of employee contributions up to 4% of annual salary, with immediate vesting. Employees are eligible to enroll in the 401(k) plan after 60 days of employment. Meridian also offers a $50,000 basic life insurance policy at no cost to employees, with the option to purchase supplemental life insurance up to 5x annual salary. Short-term disability coverage replaces 60% of salary for up to 12 weeks, and long-term disability replaces 60% of salary after a 90-day elimination period. An Employee Stock Appreciation Rights program is available to employees after 2 years of service. Open enrollment is held each November for benefits effective January 1. Contact benefits@meridianconsulting.com with questions.

title: Workplace Dress Code Policy
content:
Meridian Consulting Group maintains a business casual dress code in our offices to reflect our professional culture while allowing employees reasonable comfort and personal expression. Business casual attire includes collared shirts, blouses, sweaters, dress pants, khakis, skirts of appropriate length (at or below the knee), and closed-toe shoes. Jeans are permitted on Fridays and during internal-only workdays, provided they are clean, free of rips or distressing, and paired with a professional top. Athletic wear, flip-flops, graphic t-shirts with offensive content, and excessively casual clothing are not appropriate for the office on any day.

On days when employees are scheduled for client meetings, presentations, or site visits, business professional attire is expected. This includes suits, blazers, dress shirts or blouses, ties (optional but encouraged for client-facing roles), and polished dress shoes. Employees should be aware that some client sites may have their own dress requirements, which will be communicated by the project lead at least 48 hours before a visit. Remote employees are expected to maintain a professional appearance during video calls with clients or during all-hands meetings. A clean, uncluttered background is also required for client-facing video calls. Employees who are uncertain whether an outfit is appropriate may consult with their manager or HR Coordinator Jamie Lin. Violations of the dress code will first be addressed informally; repeated violations may be documented and included in performance records. This policy was last reviewed and updated on March 1, 2024.

title: Social Media Usage Policy
content:
Meridian Consulting Group recognizes that employees use social media platforms for personal and professional purposes. This policy establishes clear guidelines to protect the company's reputation, client confidentiality, and employee wellbeing while respecting individuals' rights to free expression. Employees are welcome to identify themselves as Meridian employees on platforms such as LinkedIn, Twitter/X, and others, but must make clear that their opinions are their own and do not represent the company's official position. When discussing work-related topics publicly, employees must not disclose any client names, project details, proprietary methodologies, financial information, or internal personnel matters. Meridian's Client Confidentiality Agreement, signed during onboarding, applies fully to social media activity.

Employees are prohibited from posting content that is harassing, discriminatory, or defamatory toward colleagues, clients, competitors, or any individual. This includes content shared on personal accounts that could reasonably be connected to the employee's role at Meridian. Employees who manage official Meridian social media accounts (currently LinkedIn, Instagram, and X, managed by the Marketing team in coordination with HR) must obtain approval from Marketing Director Priya Nambiar before publishing any content related to company culture, hiring, or employee spotlights. Violations of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination, depending on the severity of the breach and whether client confidentiality was compromised. Employees who are unsure whether a post is appropriate should consult their manager or email compliance@meridianconsulting.com before posting. This policy is reviewed annually each February.

title: Remote Work Policy Guidelines
content:
Meridian Consulting Group supports flexible work arrangements as part of our commitment to employee well-being and productivity. All full-time employees are eligible for a hybrid remote work schedule after successfully completing their 90-day introductory period. The standard hybrid model allows employees to work remotely up to 3 days per week, with a minimum of 2 required in-office days on Tuesday and Thursday, which are designated as core collaboration days. Employees in their first 90 days of employment are expected to work on-site 4 days per week to facilitate onboarding and relationship-building. Exceptions to the standard schedule may be requested through the BambooHR flex-work request form and require approval from the employee's direct manager and HR Director Sandra Kowalski.

Employees working remotely are responsible for maintaining a professional, distraction-free work environment with reliable internet connectivity (minimum 25 Mbps download speed recommended). Meridian provides each employee with a company-issued laptop and a one-time remote work stipend of $500 upon transitioning to hybrid status to cover peripheral equipment such as monitors, keyboards, or ergonomic accessories. Employees are required to be reachable via Slack and company email during core business hours of 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM in their local time zone and must attend all scheduled meetings via video. Employees seeking fully remote arrangements due to relocation or medical necessity must submit a formal request to HR at least 30 days in advance. Fully remote arrangements are evaluated on a case-by-case basis and may require role-specific justification. All remote workers must comply with Meridian's Information Security Policy and use the company VPN when accessing internal systems.

title: Interview Process and Guidelines
content:
Meridian Consulting Group is committed to a fair, consistent, and legally compliant hiring process. All open positions are posted internally for a minimum of 5 business days before external recruitment begins. The standard interview process for mid-level consulting roles consists of four stages: (1) a 30-minute phone screen conducted by HR Coordinator Jamie Lin to assess cultural fit and basic qualifications; (2) a 60-minute competency-based interview with the hiring manager focusing on technical skills and past project experience; (3) a case study presentation (45 minutes plus 15-minute Q&A) evaluated by a two-person panel; and (4) a 30-minute values and fit conversation with Managing Partner Elena Torres. Senior-level hires and Director-level positions may include an additional stakeholder panel.

All interviewers must complete Meridian's Structured Interviewing Training module in TalentLMS before participating in any interview panel. Interview questions are standardized per role and pre-approved by HR to ensure consistency and legal compliance. Interviewers are prohibited from asking about age, marital status, national origin, religion, disability, pregnancy, or any other protected characteristic. Candidate feedback must be submitted in Greenhouse (our ATS) within 24 hours of each interview using the structured scorecard. Offer decisions require consensus from the hiring manager and HR Director, with compensation parameters established before the offer is extended. Reference checks—minimum two professional references—are completed by HR before any written offer is made. Meridian aims to complete the full interview process within 3 weeks of initial application receipt. All candidate data is retained for 12 months per our record-keeping policy.

title: Diversity and Inclusion Initiatives
content:
Meridian Consulting Group is committed to building a diverse, equitable, and inclusive workplace where every employee can contribute and thrive. Our current DEI initiatives are overseen by the DEI Committee, co-chaired by HR Director Sandra Kowalski and Senior Consultant Marcus Webb, with representation from each practice area.
